message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="team-lead" summary="Respawn Luca Palmieri as Driver for Nav + Dashboard">
You are Luca Palmieri, Lead Rust Engineer on this team.

# Your Profile
You are Luca Palmieri, author of "Zero to Production in Rust." You are the lead Rust engineer. You guide the team on Rust idioms, ecosystem choices, architecture patterns, and production readiness. You ensure the codebase is correct, maintainable, observable, and deployable.

Your core philosophy: Production-ready from day one, layered architecture, comprehensive testing strategy, type-safe error handling, configuration management, observability.

Your communication style: precise, methodical, thorough. You think in terms of production systems and what happens when things go wrong.

# Your Role This Session
You are the **Driver** for this task. You are the ONLY agent who may modify files (Edit, Write, Bash). You will implement Step 1 of the GTD system: Navigation + Dashboard skeleton.

# First Steps
1. Read `PROJECT.md` and `TEAM_AGREEMENTS.md` to understand project conventions
2. Read `docs/gtd-product-discovery.md` for the full product discovery (especially sections 5 and 7)
3. Read `docs/user-stories.md` for US-GTD-8 (Navigation and Dashboard) acceptance criteria
4. Read the team config at `~/.claude/teams/gtd-evolution/config.json` to know your teammates
5. Check `TaskList` to see your assigned task (#11)
6. Review the existing codebase: src/routes/, src/startup.rs, templates/, static/main.css
7. Begin implementation following TDD and the Atomic Green Step pipeline

# Task: Implementation Step 1 — Navigation + Dashboard Skeleton

Build the multi-page GTD navigation structure and dashboard skeleton:

1. Header nav with links: Inbox, Next Actions, Projects, Waiting For, Someday/Maybe, Weekly Review
2. aria-current="page" for active nav state
3. Quick capture input in header (POST /inbox) — form that captures to inbox
4. Route skeleton for all GTD pages (placeholder content initially)
5. Dashboard/home page with summary placeholders
6. Update base.html template with new nav structure
7. Content max-width bumped to 44rem
8. hx-boost="true" on body element
9. All existing auth flows continue to work (register, login, logout)
10. Mobile-first flexbox wrap nav

Key team decisions already made:
- Header nav (not sidebar) — wrapping flexbox, single <nav> landmark
- aria-current="page" for active state (bottom border accent + semibold)
- Inline visible counts: "Inbox (3)" — placeholder for now
- hx-boost="true" on <body>
- Content max-width to 44rem
- Sign out stays as form/button, not a link
- Quick capture on every authenticated page via header input

# Important Rules
- Follow the Atomic Green Step pipeline from TEAM_AGREEMENTS.md
- TDD: Write failing tests first, then make them pass
- Wait for consensus from all 9 team members before pushing
- Commit after every green step
- You are the sole file modifier — other agents are Reviewers
- Send updates to teammates as you make progress
</teammate-message>

content.text: I'll start by reading the project documents and understanding the current codebase before implementing anything.

file.content:
# Todo List Application

A web-based, multi-user todo list application built with Rust (backend), HTMX with
server-rendered templates (frontend interactions), and TypeScript for non-critical
progressive enhancements (drag-and-drop, animations).

> **This document contains project owner constraints.** The team must follow these rules.
> Changes to this document require project owner approval. For team-owned practices and
> conventions, see `TEAM_AGREEMENTS.md`.

## Tech Stack

- **Backend**: Rust (nightly) — web framework, domain logic, HTML rendering
- **Frontend**: HTMX for server-driven interactivity, CSS for styling, TypeScript
  for progressive enhancement only
- **Templates**: Server-rendered HTML templates (Askama or similar)
- **Database**: PostgreSQL with SQLx (compile-time checked queries)
- **Authentication**: Email/password with session-based auth
- **Dev Environment**: Nix flake + direnv

## Development Mandates

These are non-negotiable practices for the project:

- **Test-Driven Development**: Every feature is built using strict TDD
  (Red-Green-Refactor). No production code without a failing test.
- **Mob/Ensemble Programming**: All production code is written by the mob. No solo
  commits to production code.
- **Consensus Decision-Making**: The team operates by consensus. No single technical
  lead or decision-maker. If consensus is not reached after 10 rounds of substantive
  discussion, the decision is escalated to the project owner for a final call.
- **Driver-Reviewer Mob Model**: At most one agent (the Driver) may modify files at
  any time. The remaining eight agents participate as Reviewers via read-only access
  and messaging. All nine agents must reach consensus before a task is considered
  complete.
- **Code Quality Gates**: `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`, `cargo fmt`, and all tests
  must pass before any commit. Structured logging with `tracing` from day one.

## Environment & Tooling

### Nix

This project runs on NixOS with a `flake.nix` already in place. When the team needs
a utility that isn't currently installed:
- Use `nix shell nixpkgs#<package> --command <cmd>` for one-off commands
- For tools used regularly, add them to `buildInputs` in the devshell in `flake.nix`

### Agent Skills

The team is encouraged to find and install agent skills to make their work easier via
`npx skills`. The `find-skill` skill is already installed. When adding skills:
- Add them for **this project only** (not globally)
- Use support for **generic agent locations** (Codex, OpenCode, etc.) **and** Claude Code

### Custom Skills

When the team finds an approach that works and was not completely obvious (anything
that took a few tries to figure out), and there is not already an existing skill
available (after searching with `find-skill`), the team **should** create a
**local-only, project-specific skill** using the `skills.sh` format. This ensures
hard-won knowledge is captured and reusable.

### External Services (Docker)

If the team needs any external services running (e.g., a PostgreSQL database server),
use a `docker-compose.yml` file and Docker to run them. Do **not** run services
directly on the host machine. This keeps the dev environment reproducible and
self-contained.

## MVP Scope (Completed)

The original MVP (simple todo list) has been completed. All features below are shipped
and working:

- User registration (email/password)
- User login/logout
- View personal todo list
- Add a todo item
- Complete a todo item (toggle)
- Delete a todo item
- Edit a todo item title

## V1 Scope: Getting Things Done (GTD) System

The product is evolving from a simple todo list into a full GTD (Getting Things Done)
system. See `docs/gtd-product-discovery.md` for the complete product discovery document.

### Must Have
- **Inbox capture**: Quick capture of anything on the user's mind, minimal friction
- **Clarify workflow**: Process inbox items through GTD decision tree (actionable?
  next action? project? delegate? defer? trash?)
- **Next Actions list with contexts**: Actions organized by context (@computer, @home,
  @errands, etc.) so users work from context-appropriate lists
- **Projects list**: Track multi-step outcomes; each project has at least one next action;
  stalled projects are flagged
- **Waiting For list**: Track delegated/blocked items with who/what and date
- **Someday/Maybe list**: Park ideas without cluttering action lists
- **Weekly Review guided flow**: Three-phase review (Get Clear, Get Current, Get Creative)
  to keep the system trustworthy
- **GTD navigation and dashboard**: Navigate between all GTD lists; dashboard shows
  inbox count, stalled projects, action counts
- **Complete and delete**: Mark actions done, remove irrelevant items across all lists

### Should Have
- Project support materials (notes attached to projects)
- Move items between lists (reclassify as understanding changes)

### Could Have
- Review scheduling (remind when weekly review is due)
- Bulk inbox processing

### Out of Scope (Future)
- Calendar / date-specific items (requires date picker, time zones, recurring events)
- Reference file storage (needs file upload, search, storage infrastructure)
- Horizons of Focus / goals / vision (higher-level planning)
- Natural language input ("call Bob tomorrow")
- Email-to-inbox capture (requires email infrastructure)
- Mobile native app (responsive web is sufficient)
- Sharing / delegation to other users (multi-user collaboration)
- Tags beyond contexts
- Search and filter
- Dark mode
- Drag-and-drop reordering
- Recurring items
- Social login (OAuth)

content.content:
1→# Team Agreements
     2→
     3→*Formed: Day 0 -- Team Formation Session*
     4→*Facilitator: Kent Beck*
     5→*Status: APPROVED -- Consensus reached*
     6→
     7→---
     8→
     9→## 1. Working Agreements
    10→
    11→### Core Principles
    12→- **Outcomes over output**: We measure success by user problems solved, not features
    13→  shipped. Before starting any feature, the team articulates the user problem it solves
    14→  and how we will know it is working (success criteria).
    15→- **MVP discipline is sacred**: Any idea beyond the defined MVP scope gets acknowledged,
    16→  written on the Future List (`docs/future-ideas.md`), and deferred. The answer to
    17→  "wouldn't it be cool if..." is "Yes -- in v2." Scope expansion requires team consensus,
    18→  and Marty will push back hard unless there is a compelling discovery that changes our
    19→  understanding of the user problem.
    20→- **User stories are conversations, not contracts**: User stories guide our work but are
    21→  owned by the whole team. Anyone can propose changes to acceptance criteria with good
    22→  reasoning.
    23→
    24→### Communication
    25→- We communicate openly, directly, and respectfully.
    26→- Every team member's perspective is valued equally -- the accessibility specialist's
    27→  concerns carry the same weight as the lead engineer's.
    28→- When raising a concern, propose an alternative. "I don't like X" is less useful than
    29→  "I'd prefer Y because Z."
    30→- We ask questions rather than make assumptions -- especially across disciplines.
    31→
    32→### Driver-Reviewer Mob Model
    33→- **Full 9-person mob is always active.** All team members participate in every task.
    34→- **One Driver, eight Reviewers**: The Driver is the sole agent who may modify files
    35→  (using Edit, Write, and Bash tools). All other agents operate as Reviewers -- they
    36→  read code, review changes, and communicate feedback via messages only.
    37→- **Non-driver agents MUST NOT use Edit, Write, or Bash tools that modify files.**
    38→  Violation is treated as a process failure.
    39→- **Driver rotates by task**: The Driver is chosen based on the expertise needed for
    40→  each task. The coordinator designates the Driver when assigning work.
    41→- **Reviewers focus on their specialty**: Each Reviewer contributes from their area of
    42→  expertise (a11y, UX, design, domain modeling, Rust patterns, HTMX, CSS, product,
    43→  TDD discipline) and sends suggestions to the Driver via messages.
    44→- **All suggestions flow through messages**: Reviewers describe intent and concerns,
    45→  not keystrokes. The Driver translates feedback into code changes.
    46→- **Everyone participates**: Designers, PM, accessibility specialist, domain architect --
    47→  every perspective matters. Different eyes catch different things.
    48→- **It's okay to say "I don't understand."** The mob is a learning environment.
    49→
    50→### Feature Workflow
    51→Each feature follows this process with the full 9-person mob:
    52→
    53→1. **Discovery**: Who is the user? What is the problem? What does "working"
    54→   look like? Identify edge cases (empty state, error state, 1000 items, special
    55→   characters). Marty leads.
    56→2. **Type sketch**: What domain types and states are involved? Quick sketch of
    57→   enums, newtypes, and workflows. Scott leads.
    58→3. **Design intent**: What is the visual hierarchy? What states need designing
    59→   (empty, loading, error, success)? Steve Schoger describes layout, spacing, and
    60→   typography in concrete terms.
    61→4. **TDD cycle**: Red-Green-Refactor. The compiler is our first test. Take the smallest
    62→   step possible. The Driver writes code; Reviewers provide feedback via messages.
    63→   Kent facilitates TDD discipline.
    64→5. **Continuous review**: Reviewers actively review each change as it happens --
    65→   accessibility checked by Heydon, usability checked by Steve Krug, design checked
    66→   by Steve Schoger, hypermedia patterns checked by Carson, domain modeling checked
    67→   by Scott, Rust patterns checked by Luca, product fit checked by Marty.
    68→6. **Consensus gate**: After each atomic green step, all 9 agents confirm the change
    69→   is correct before the Driver proceeds to the next step. The coordinator collects
    70→   consensus before marking any task complete.
    71→
    72→### Decision Cadence
    73→- Day-to-day coding decisions (naming, small refactors, local patterns) are made
    74→  naturally in the mob -- no formal process needed.
    75→- Significant decisions (architecture, patterns, library choices, UX patterns, design
    76→  system changes) use the consensus protocol defined in Section 4.
    77→
    78→---
    79→
    80→## 2. Definition of Done
    81→
    82→A feature is **done** when ALL of the following are true:
    83→
    84→### Product
    85→- [ ] The user problem is clearly solved
    86→- [ ] All acceptance criteria from the user story are met
    87→- [ ] A first-time user can accomplish the task without hesitating (Trunk Test)
    88→- [ ] User can complete a full journey from the landing page through the feature
    89→      being delivered
    90→- [ ] All navigation paths lead somewhere useful (no dead-end pages)
    91→- [ ] Edge cases are handled gracefully (not just the happy path)
    92→- [ ] The feature is deployable -- not just merged, but shippable to real users
    93→
    94→### Engineering
    95→- [ ] Tests are written first (TDD: Red-Green-Refactor) and all pass
    96→- [ ] `cargo fmt --check` passes
    97→- [ ] `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` passes with zero warnings
    98→- [ ] `cargo test` passes (unit + integration)
    99→- [ ] `npx playwright test` passes -- browser-based e2e tests pass for affected
   100→      user flows
   101→- [ ] Error cases return meaningful HTML responses with structured logging
   102→- [ ] Tracing spans cover the new functionality
   103→- [ ] Database migrations (if any) are committed and `sqlx-data.json` is updated
   104→
   105→### Accessibility (WCAG 2.2 AA)
   106→- [ ] HTML is semantic and uses correct elements (`<button>`, `<label>`, `<ul>`, etc.)
   107→- [ ] All form inputs have visible `<label>` elements (no placeholder-only inputs)
   108→- [ ] All interactive elements are fully keyboard operable
   109→- [ ] Screen reader testing (VoiceOver, NVDA, or equivalent) confirms: interactive
   110→      elements are announced correctly, state changes are communicated, and dynamic
   111→      content updates are announced via live regions
   112→- [ ] ARIA used only when native HTML semantics are insufficient (with comments explaining why)
   113→- [ ] Color contrast meets WCAG AA (4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text/UI)
   114→- [ ] Color is never the only indicator of state (always a secondary visual cue)
   115→- [ ] Focus management is correct after HTMX swaps (see HTMX Focus Protocol below)
   116→- [ ] Custom `:focus-visible` styles are present with sufficient contrast
   117→- [ ] `prefers-reduced-motion` is respected for all animations/transitions
   118→
   119→### Design
   120→- [ ] Visual design follows the established design token system
   121→- [ ] All states are designed: empty, loading, error, success
   122→- [ ] Visual hierarchy is clear -- one primary focal point per screen
   123→- [ ] Spacing is consistent and uses the defined scale
   124→
   125→### Frontend
   126→- [ ] Core functionality works via standard HTML forms and links (progressive
   127→      enhancement -- HTMX and TypeScript enhance but are not required for basic operation)
   128→- [ ] HTMX attributes follow locality of behavior (self-describing elements)
   129→- [ ] TypeScript (if any) is non-critical and has accessible alternatives
   130→
   131→### Process
   132→- [ ] The mob has continuously reviewed code, design, usability, and accessibility
   133→      during development
   134→- [ ] All 9 team members have reviewed and consent to the completed work
   135→- [ ] Labels and copy follow the Copy Standards (see Section 3)
   136→
   137→---
   138→
   139→## 3. Code Conventions and Patterns
   140→
   141→### Project Structure
   142→
   143→```
   144→src/
   145→  main.rs              -- Startup, configuration loading, server binding
   146→  lib.rs               -- Re-exports for integration test access
   147→  configuration.rs     -- Config structs, environment-aware loading
   148→  startup.rs           -- Application builder (Router, DB pool, middleware)
   149→  routes/              -- HTTP handlers (thin: extract, delegate, respond)
   150→    mod.rs
   151→    health_check.rs
   152→  domain/              -- Pure domain types and logic (NO I/O)
   153→    mod.rs
   154→  services/            -- Application services (orchestrate domain + infra)
   155→    mod.rs
   156→  infrastructure/      -- Database queries, external service clients
   157→    mod.rs
   158→  templates/           -- Askama HTML templates
   159→tests/
   160→  api/                 -- Integration tests against the full HTTP stack
   161→    mod.rs
   162→    helpers.rs         -- TestApp, spawn_app(), shared fixtures
   163→    health_check.rs
   164→```
   165→
   166→Dependencies flow inward: routes -> services -> domain + infrastructure.
   167→Domain depends on nothing. This makes the domain layer trivially testable.
   168→
   169→### Rust Conventions
   170→
   171→**Type-driven domain modeling:**
   172→- Newtypes for all domain identifiers and constrained values: `UserId(Uuid)`,
   173→  `Email(String)`, `Title(String)`, `TodoItemId(Uuid)`
   174→- Enums for state machines -- make illegal states unrepresentable:
   175→  ```rust
   176→  // GOOD: the type system prevents invalid states
   177→  enum TodoItem {
   178→      Pending { title: Title, created_at: DateTime<Utc> },
   179→      Completed { title: Title, created_at: DateTime<Utc>, completed_at: DateTime<Utc> },
   180→  }
   181→  ```
   182→- **Parse, don't validate**: Instead of `fn validate(input: &str) -> bool`, write
   183→  `fn parse(input: String) -> Result<Email, ValidationError>`. Validation that returns
   184→  a boolean throws away information. Parsing produces a typed value that carries the
   185→  proof of validity. Validate once at the boundary; never re-validate inside the system.
   186→
   187→**Workflows as type-safe pipelines:**
   188→```
   189→UnvalidatedInput -> ValidatedInput -> DomainEvent -> PersistenceResult
   190→```
   191→Each step's output type is the next step's input. You cannot skip validation or
   192→persist before validating.
   193→
   194→**Error handling (three layers):**
   195→- **Domain errors**: Custom enum with `thiserror`. Domain terms only -- no HTTP concepts,
   196→  no infrastructure types. A `TodoError::TitleEmpty` must never contain a `sqlx::Error`.
   197→  Domain errors and infrastructure errors are separate types, translated at the boundary.
   198→  ```rust
   199→  #[derive(Debug, thiserror::Error)]
   200→  pub enum TodoError {
   201→      #[error("Todo title cannot be empty")]
   202→      TitleEmpty,
   203→      #[error("Todo title too long (max {max}, got {actual})")]
   204→      TitleTooLong { max: usize, actual: usize },
   205→      #[error("Todo not found")]
   206→      NotFound { id: TodoItemId },
   207→      #[error(transparent)]
   208→      Unexpected(#[from] anyhow::Error),
   209→  }
   210→  ```
   211→- **Service errors**: Map domain errors to actionable responses. Add context.
   212→- **Route errors**: Implement `IntoResponse` for error types. Return meaningful HTML
   213→  (not raw 500s). Every error path produces a user-facing response.
   214→- **Always add context** when propagating errors. A bare `?` that loses context is a
   215→  code smell.
   216→- **Log at error boundaries**: Every error crossing an architectural boundary gets
   217→  logged with structured `tracing` fields.
   218→
   219→**Idiomatic Rust:**
   220→- Prefer iterators over loops, pattern matching over conditionals
   221→- `?` operator for error propagation with context
   222→- `#[must_use]` on important return types
   223→- Compile-time guarantees: SQLx `query!`/`query_as!` macros, Askama templates
   224→
   225→**Testing strategy (TDD):**
   226→- **Unit tests** (`#[cfg(test)] mod tests`): Pure domain logic, validation, type
   227→  conversions. Fast, no I/O, no mocking. **Litmus test: if you need a mock to test
   228→  domain logic, I/O has leaked into the domain layer. Push it back to the boundary.**
   229→- **Integration tests** (`tests/` directory): Full HTTP stack with real database.
   230→  `TestApp` struct with `spawn_app()` helper. Unique database per test run
   231→  (random name, run migrations, drop after).
   232→- **No mocks for the database**: Use real PostgreSQL via Docker. Mocks give false
   233→  confidence.
   234→- **Test names as behavior specifications**:
   235→  ```rust
   236→  #[tokio::test]
   237→  async fn registration_returns_422_when_email_is_missing() { ... }
   238→  ```
   239→- **Acceptance tests as usability statements**: "When I type a todo and press Enter,
   240→  it appears in my list." Tests describe what the user expects to happen.
   241→- **Property-based tests** with `proptest` where appropriate.
   242→- **End-to-end tests** (`e2e/` directory): Playwright browser tests against the
   243→  running application. Cover critical user journeys (register, login, core feature,
   244→  logout). Use role-based and label-based locators (`getByRole`, `getByLabel`) so
   245→  tests double as accessibility assertions. Keep this layer thin -- only add e2e
   246→  tests for flows that integration tests cannot adequately verify.
   247→- **Accessibility testing** (four layers):
   248→  - *Automated*: axe-core (or equivalent) in the test suite for missing labels,
   249→    contrast violations, and structural issues
   250→  - *Keyboard*: Manual keyboard navigation testing for every interactive flow
   251→  - *Screen reader*: Manual VoiceOver/NVDA testing for every feature
   252→  - *Structural*: HTML validation for semantic correctness
   253→  Tests can assert on roles, labels, and ARIA attributes -- documenting the expected
   254→  screen reader experience as living specifications.
   255→
   256→### Initial Crate List
   257→
   258→Proposed starting dependencies (open to mob discussion):
   259→
   260→| Crate | Purpose |
   261→|-------|---------|
   262→| `axum` | Web framework |
   263→| `tokio` (full features) | Async runtime |
   264→| `sqlx` (postgres, migrate, runtime-tokio-rustls) | Database |
   265→| `askama` + `askama_axum` | Templates |
   266→| `serde` + `serde_json` | Serialization |
   267→| `tracing` + `tracing-subscriber` (env-filter) | Observability |
   268→| `thiserror` | Domain error types |
   269→| `anyhow` | Ad-hoc error context |
   270→| `argon2` | Password hashing |
   271→| `tower-sessions` + `tower-sessions-sqlx-store` | Session management |
   272→| `uuid` | Identifiers |
   273→
   274→Note: Input validation will be handled through custom newtypes with validation in
   275→constructors (Scott's approach), not a validation framework. The types carry the
   276→proof of validity.
   277→
   278→### HTML / HTMX Conventions
   279→
   280→**The Hypermedia Contract:**
   281→- **Return HTML, not JSON.** Every UI endpoint returns HTML. No JSON API layer.
   282→- **Semantic HTML first**: `<button>` for actions, `<a>` for navigation, `<form>` for
   283→  data submission, `<ul>`/`<ol>` for lists. Never `<div onclick>`.
   284→- **Form accessibility**: Every input has a visible `<label>` (never placeholder-only).
   285→  Validation errors are associated with inputs via `aria-describedby` and announced
   286→  via `aria-live="polite"` regions. Use `aria-live="assertive"` only for critical errors.
   287→- **Locality of behavior**: Every HTMX-enhanced element is self-describing. You
   288→  understand what it does by looking at it alone.
   289→- **No client-side state**: The DOM is the state, the server is the source of truth.
   290→  No client-side data stores. The only acceptable client-side state is transient UI
   291→  state for progressive enhancement (e.g., drag position).
   292→
   293→**Server endpoint conventions:**
   294→- **Full page requests** (no `HX-Request` header): Return complete HTML page with
   295→  layout, nav, landmarks.
   296→- **HTMX requests** (with `HX-Request` header): Return just the HTML fragment for
   297→  the swap.
   298→- **`HX-Trigger` response header** for cross-component updates (e.g., updating a
   299→  todo count after deletion).
   300→- **Same URL, same handler**: One handler checks the `HX-Request` header to decide
   301→  whether to return a full page or a fragment. No separate `/api/` routes.
   302→- **HTTP methods used semantically**: GET = read, POST = create, PUT/PATCH = update,
   303→  DELETE = delete.
   304→- **Status codes**: 200 for success with HTML, 422 for validation errors with error
   305→  HTML, 200 with empty body for deletion (allows HTMX swap to remove element).
   306→
   307→**Progressive enhancement (non-negotiable):**
   308→- The app MUST work without JavaScript. Forms submit, links navigate, content is
   309→  readable with HTML alone.
   310→- HTMX enhances the experience (partial updates, no full page reloads).
   311→- TypeScript is only for things neither HTML nor HTMX can do.
   312→
   313→**HTMX Focus Management Protocol:**
   314→After every HTMX swap:
   315→- If an element was **removed**: Move focus to the logical next element.
   316→- If content was **updated in place**: Keep focus where it was.
   317→- Use `aria-live="polite"` regions to announce state changes to screen readers.
   318→- Test every HTMX interaction with Tab key before considering it done.
   319→
   320→**Page structure (every page):**
   321→- Skip link to main content (first focusable element)
   322→- Landmark regions: `<main>`, `<nav>`, `<header>`, `<footer>`
   323→- Logical heading hierarchy: one `<h1>` per page, no skipped levels
   324→- Descriptive `<title>` element
   325→
   326→### CSS Conventions
   327→
   328→**Progressive enhancement layers:**
   329→1. **Layer 1 (HTML)**: Functional and readable with zero CSS.
   330→2. **Layer 2 (CSS)**: Beautiful -- layout, typography, color, transitions, focus styles.
   331→3. **Layer 3 (TypeScript)**: Delightful -- drag-and-drop, advanced keyboard shortcuts.
   332→
   333→**CSS organization with cascade layers:**
   334→```css
   335→@layer reset, tokens, base, layout, components, utilities;
   336→```
   337→- **reset**: Normalize/reset styles
   338→- **tokens**: Custom properties (design tokens)
   339→- **base**: Element-level styles (body, headings, links, forms)
   340→- **layout**: Page-level layout (CSS Grid)
   341→- **components**: Component-specific styles (todo-item, auth-form)
   342→- **utilities**: Helper classes (visually-hidden, etc.)
   343→- No `!important` unless in the utilities layer for intentional overrides.
   344→- CSS nesting is fine for component-scoped styles but avoid nesting deeper than
   345→  2 levels.
   346→
   347→**Design tokens (CSS custom properties on `:root`):**
   348→- **Spacing scale** (4px base): 4, 8, 12, 16, 24, 32, 48, 64, 96 -- encoded as
   349→  `--space-xs` through `--space-3xl`
   350→- **Color palette**: oklch color space for perceptual uniformity. One primary color,
   351→  neutral gray palette (50-900), semantic colors (danger, success, warning). Most UI
   352→  is grayscale; color is for emphasis and interaction.
   353→- **Typography**: System font stack (`system-ui, sans-serif`). Type scale from xs to
   354→  3xl with defined line-height. Tighter for headings, looser for body.
   355→- **Shadows**: Three levels (sm, md, lg). Never combine shadows and borders on the
   356→  same element.
   357→- **Border radius**: Three sizes (sm, md, lg).
   358→- **All values reference tokens -- no hardcoded hex values or pixel values in
   359→  component styles.**
   360→
   361→**Design tokens are a prerequisite**: The token system (spacing, colors, typography,
   362→shadows, radii) must be established and agreed upon before any feature UI work begins.
   363→This is foundational -- without it, inconsistent one-off values accumulate from day one.
   364→
   365→**Design token governance**: Adding a new color, spacing value, or typography level
   366→goes through the mob (Steve Schoger approves design token changes).
   367→
   368→**Design token flow**: Steve Schoger defines the visual design -> Lea encodes as CSS
   369→custom properties -> Components reference only tokens -> Themes/dark mode reassign
   370→properties. No build step. No JSON-to-CSS pipeline.
   371→
   372→**Modern CSS features:**
   373→- `:has()` for parent-based styling (e.g., `li:has(input:checked)` for completed todos)
   374→- Container queries where components need to respond to container size
   375→- Logical properties (`margin-inline`, `padding-block`) for future RTL support
   376→- `clamp()` for fluid typography
   377→- View Transitions API for smooth HTMX swap animations
   378→- `@supports` for graceful degradation of newer features
   379→
   380→**Accessibility in CSS:**
   381→- Custom `:focus-visible` styles with at least 3:1 contrast. Never `outline: none`
   382→  without a replacement.
   383→- `prefers-reduced-motion`: All animations/transitions wrapped in a motion query.
   384→  Reduced or no motion as the alternative.
   385→- `prefers-color-scheme`: Supported if/when the team decides to add dark mode.
   386→- Color is never the only state indicator.
   387→
   388→**Responsive design**: Mobile-first. Default styles target the smallest viewport;
   389→enhancements added via `min-width` media queries. Breakpoints: mobile (default),
   390→desktop (~1024px). A todo list should work beautifully on a phone.
   391→
   392→**Browser targets**: Last 2 versions of evergreen browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari,
   393→Edge). No IE11. `@supports` for graceful degradation. No polyfills unless necessary.
   394→
   395→### TypeScript Conventions
   396→- **No framework**: Vanilla TypeScript with DOM APIs.
   397→- **Progressive enhancement pattern**: Feature works without JS, TypeScript enhances.
   398→- **Event delegation**: Listeners on parent containers, not individual elements.
   399→- **State via data attributes**: TypeScript toggles `data-*` attributes or classes;
   400→  CSS handles all visual changes based on those.
   401→- **No CSS-in-JS**: Styles live in CSS files. TypeScript never sets inline styles
   402→  (except computed values like drag position).
   403→- **Thin layer**: If a TypeScript file is getting large, we are probably doing
   404→  something CSS or HTMX could handle.
   405→
   406→### Copy Standards
   407→- **Labels**: 1-2 words when possible.
   408→- **Error messages**: Plain language. Say what went wrong, suggest what to do next.
   409→  "That email isn't registered -- want to create an account?" not "Error: User not found."
   410→- **Button labels**: Describe the action ("Add todo", "Sign in"), not vague labels
   411→  ("Submit", "OK").
   412→- **No happy talk**: Every word earns its place or gets cut. No introductory paragraphs
   413→  that say nothing useful.
   414→- **Design for scanning, not reading**: Users don't read pages, they scan. Clear
   415→  headings, short text, prominent calls to action, no walls of text.
   416→- **Cut the text, then cut it again**: If you wrote a paragraph, ask "can I say this
   417→  in half the words?"
   418→
   419→### Domain Language and Modeling Conventions
   420→- Code uses the ubiquitous language of the domain. "Complete a task" ->
   421→  `complete_task()`, not `update_status()`.
   422→- Domain types are explicit -- no primitive obsession. Every domain concept gets its
   423→  own type, even if it is "just a string" or "just a UUID" underneath.
   424→- Newtypes are constructed only through validated factory methods (the `parse`
   425→  pattern). You cannot create a `TodoTitle` from an arbitrary string without going
   426→  through validation.
   427→- Every `is_*` boolean flag is a candidate for an enum. Ask: "what states does this
   428→  represent?" and model them explicitly.
   429→- Maintain a domain glossary in `docs/glossary.md` mapping business terms to types:
   430→
   431→  | Domain Term | Rust Type | Notes |
   432→  |-------------|-----------|-------|
   433→  | User | `User` | Authenticated user with valid credentials |
   434→  | Email | `ValidatedEmail` | Constructed via `parse`, not `new` |
   435→  | Todo item | `TodoItem` (enum) | `Pending` or `Completed` variant |
   436→  | Todo title | `TodoTitle` | Non-empty, max length, validated at construction |
   437→
   438→### Observability
   439→
   440→**Structured logging with `tracing`:**
   441→- Request ID (UUID) attached to every request via middleware.
   442→- Span fields: `request_id`, `method`, `uri`, `status_code`.
   443→- Application-level spans for significant operations: `register_user`,
   444→  `authenticate`, `create_todo`, etc.
   445→- Use `#[tracing::instrument]` for service functions.
   446→- Structured fields on errors: what failed, why, relevant IDs.
   447→
   448→**Log level conventions:**
   449→| Level | When to use |
   450→|-------|-------------|
   451→| `ERROR` | Something is broken, needs human attention |
   452→| `WARN` | Unexpected but handled (e.g., invalid login attempt) |
   453→| `INFO` | Significant business events (user registered, todo created) |
   454→| `DEBUG` | Technical details useful for debugging |
   455→| `TRACE` | Very verbose (request/response bodies, SQL queries) |
   456→
   457→### Database Conventions
   458→- **Migrations**: SQLx migrations (`sqlx migrate`), numbered SQL files, version controlled.
   459→- **Compile-time checked queries**: `sqlx::query!` and `sqlx::query_as!` macros.
   460→- **Offline mode for CI**: `sqlx-data.json` generated via `cargo sqlx prepare`,
   461→  committed to version control.
   462→- **Connection pool**: `PgPool` shared via Axum state.
   463→- **UUIDs for primary keys**: `uuid` type in PostgreSQL, `Uuid` in Rust.
   464→- **Timestamps**: `timestamptz` in PostgreSQL, `chrono::DateTime<Utc>` or
   465→  `time::OffsetDateTime` in Rust.
   466→- **Naming conventions**: snake_case for tables and columns. Plural table names
   467→  (`users`, `todos`).
   468→
   469→### Configuration Management
   470→- Layered configuration: `base.yaml` (defaults) -> `local.yaml` (gitignored overrides)
   471→  -> environment variables (12-factor style).
   472→- **Fail fast**: Missing required configuration at startup panics with a clear message.
   473→  No silent defaults for critical settings (database URL, secret keys).
   474→- Separate configuration structs: `DatabaseSettings`, `ApplicationSettings`,
   475→  `AuthSettings`.
   476→
   477→### CI/CD Quality Gates
   478→
   479→Every commit must pass (in order):
   480→1. `cargo fmt --check`
   481→2. `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`
   482→3. `cargo test` (unit + integration)
   483→4. `npx playwright test` (browser-based e2e)
   484→5. `cargo audit` (dependency vulnerabilities)
   485→
   486→### Atomic Green Step Pipeline
   487→
   488→*Every change follows this pipeline. No shortcuts, no skipped steps.*
   489→
   490→1. **Write code** — the smallest meaningful change.
   491→2. **`cargo fmt --check`** — fix formatting if needed.
   492→3. **`cargo clippy -- -D warnings`** — fix warnings if needed.
   493→4. **`cargo test`** — fix failures if needed.
   494→5. **`npx playwright test`** — fix e2e failures if needed.
   495→6. **`git commit`** — commit locally. **Do NOT push yet.**
   496→7. **All 9 agents review and reach consensus** — Driver and Reviewers confirm the change
   497→   is correct. If concerns are raised, the Driver fixes them, re-runs steps 2–6, and
   498→   re-seeks consensus.
   499→8. **Once 9/9 consensus → `git push`.**
   500→9. **`gh run list --limit 1`** — verify CI completes green.
   501→10. **Only then begin the next change.**
   502→
   503→Additional rules:
   504→- **Consensus before push.** Code is reviewed locally before it reaches the remote. This
   505→  keeps the CI wait out of the feedback loop.
   506→- **If CI fails, fix it before writing new code.** A red CI is the team's top priority.
   507→  No new features, no new tests, no refactoring until CI is green again.
   508→- **One Driver at a time.** Only the designated Driver may modify files. Non-driver agents
   509→  MUST NOT use Edit, Write, or Bash tools that modify files. Violation is treated as a
   510→  process failure.
   511→
   512→### Dependency Management
   513→
   514→Before adding a new crate:
   515→- Is it well-maintained? (Recent commits, responsive maintainers)
   516→- Is it widely used? (Downloads, reverse dependencies)
   517→- Compatible license? (MIT/Apache-2.0 preferred)
   518→- Can we avoid it? (Prefer stdlib or existing dependencies)
   519→
   520→### Documentation
   521→- **Code comments**: Only where "why" is not obvious from the code.
   522→- **ADRs**: For significant technical decisions. Format: context, decision,
   523→  consequences. Stored in `docs/adr/`.
   524→- **Domain glossary**: `docs/glossary.md` mapping business terms to code.
   525→- **README**: Practical -- how to set up, run tests, run the app.
   526→
   527→---
   528→
   529→## 4. Disagreement Resolution
   530→
   531→### In the Mob (Day-to-Day)
   532→1. State the disagreement clearly: "I think X, you think Y."
   533→2. Each side explains their reasoning briefly (< 2 minutes each).
   534→3. If possible, **try both and let the code decide** -- write a quick test or prototype.
   535→4. If one approach is simpler, prefer it (smallest viable decision).
   536→5. If still stuck after 5 minutes, the mob votes. Majority wins for day-to-day decisions.
   537→   The dissenter can say "I disagree but won't block."
   538→
   539→### For Significant Decisions (Consensus Protocol)
   540→1. Any team member raises the decision point with a proposal and reasoning.
   541→2. Each team member shares their perspective (concerns, alternatives, support).
   542→3. Discussion continues until:
   543→   - **Consensus**: Everyone agrees or consents ("I can live with this").
   544→   - **Compromise**: A synthesized solution addresses all concerns.
   545→   - **Stand-aside**: "I disagree but won't block" -- recorded and respected.
   546→4. If no consensus after **10 rounds** of substantive discussion, **escalate to the
   547→   project owner** with:
   548→   - Options considered
   549→   - Each member's position
   550→   - Trade-offs
   551→   - Team recommendation (if possible)
   552→
   553→### When to Involve the Project Owner
   554→- Consensus deadlock after 10 rounds
   555→- Any proposed change to MVP scope
   556→- Questions about target user or business context the team cannot answer
   557→- Major architectural pivots that significantly change the timeline
   558→- Brief status updates at milestones (keep the owner informed, don't go dark)
   559→
   560→### Principles
   561→- Disagree with the idea, not the person.
   562→- Once decided, **disagree and commit** -- no undermining, no "I told you so."
   563→- Revisit decisions when new information emerges (from tests, users, or production).
   564→- **Write it down**: Major decisions recorded as Architecture Decision Records (ADRs)
   565→  in `docs/adr/`.
   566→
   567→---
   568→
   569→## 5. Architectural Principles
   570→
   571→1. **Simplicity first**: Choose the simplest approach that could work. Add complexity
   572→   only when tests or user feedback demand it. "Could this be simpler?" is always a
   573→   valid question.
   574→2. **Server-side state**: The server is the source of truth for both data and
   575→   presentation. The browser renders what the server sends. No client-side state
   576→   management.
   577→3. **Pure domain core**: Domain logic is pure functions and types with no I/O. Side
   578→   effects live at the boundaries. This makes the domain trivially testable.
   579→4. **Make illegal states unrepresentable**: Use the type system to prevent bugs at
   580→   compile time. Newtypes, enums, and validated types carry proof of correctness.
   581→5. **Production-ready from day one**: Observability, error handling, and structured
   582→   logging from the first line of code -- not bolted on later.
   583→6. **Accessibility is a quality, not a feature**: Every feature is accessible from the
   584→   moment it is built. Inaccessible is broken. ~15-20% of users have some form of
   585→   disability -- that is not an edge case.
   586→7. **Progressive enhancement**: HTML works -> CSS makes it beautiful -> JS makes it
   587→   delightful. Each layer enhances without being required by the layers above.
   588→8. **Conventions over cleverness**: Follow established patterns. A login form should
   589→   look like every other login form. Don't make the user think. Design for scanning,
   590→   not reading. Cut unnecessary words. When in doubt, simplify.
   591→9. **Continuous testing**: TDD keeps us honest. The compiler is our first test. We
   592→   never skip the red step. Tests read like behavior specifications.
   593→10. **Incremental design**: Let the design emerge from tests and feedback. Don't
   594→    over-design upfront. Make the code a little better before adding a feature (Tidy
   595→    First), but don't redesign the universe. Ship early, learn fast.
   596→11. **Walking skeleton first**: Our first milestone is the thinnest possible slice
   597→    through the entire stack -- a user can register, log in, see an empty todo list,
   598→    add one item, and log out. Everything else builds on top.
   599→12. **Validate at the boundary, trust the types**: Input validation happens once, at
   600→    the system boundary. It produces typed values that carry proof of validity.
   601→    The interior of the system trusts its own types.
   602→
   603→---
   604→
   605→## 6. Retrospective Cadence
   606→
   607→All team members are AI agents operating continuously. Time-based retros (weekly,
   608→bi-weekly) do not apply. Instead, retros are triggered by events.
   609→
   610→- **After each feature**: Retro immediately after completing a feature. What worked?
   611→  What didn't? What should we change?
   612→- **After significant incidents**: If a build breaks, a major refactor goes sideways,
   613→  or the team gets stuck for an extended period, retro after resolution.
   614→- **Format**: Simple Start/Stop/Continue.
   615→- **Action items**: Every retro produces at most 2-3 concrete action items. These are
   616→  tracked and checked at the next retro.
   617→- **Agreements are living**: These team agreements can be updated at any retro if the
   618→  team reaches consensus.
   619→- **Usability testing**: Aim for informal testing -- three users, 20 minutes each --
   620→  at natural breakpoints. Even watching one person use the app for 5 minutes reveals
   621→  more than a week of discussion.
   622→
   623→---
   624→
   625→## 7. Tooling & Repository
   626→
   627→### Source Control
   628→- **Repository**: Public GitHub repository under the `jwilger` account
   629→- **License**: MIT
   630→- **Branching**: Work on `main` for now. Feature branches if/when the team decides
   631→  they are needed.
   632→
   633→### CI/CD
   634→- **GitHub Actions**: CI runs on every push and pull request to `main`.
   635→- **Pipeline stages** (in order):
   636→  1. `cargo fmt --check`
   637→  2. `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`
   638→  3. `cargo test`
   639→  4. `npx playwright test`
   640→- Rust nightly toolchain (matching the project's `rust-toolchain.toml`)
   641→- Node.js 22 for Playwright e2e tests
   642→- CI must pass before merging any pull request.
   643→
   644→### Work Tracking
   645→- **GitHub Issues**: For tracking features, bugs, and tasks visible to the project owner.
   646→- **Claude Task tool**: For in-progress coordination between team members during a session.
   647→- Issues and tasks should reference each other when applicable.
   648→
   649→---
   650→
   651→## 8. Team Composition Assessment
   652→
   653→### Current Team (9 members)
   654→| Name | Role | Domain |
   655→|------|------|--------|
   656→| Kent Beck | TDD Coach & Dev Practice Lead | TDD, XP, refactoring |
   657→| Scott Wlaschin | Domain Architect | DDD, type-driven design, FP |
   658→| Luca Palmieri | Lead Rust Engineer | Axum, SQLx, production Rust |
   659→| Carson Gross | Hypermedia Architect & Frontend Lead | HTMX, hypermedia systems |
   660→| Lea Verou | Frontend Engineer | CSS, web standards |
   661→| Steve Schoger | UI Designer | Visual design, design systems |
   662→| Steve Krug | UX Specialist | Usability, user advocacy |
   663→| Heydon Pickering | Accessibility Specialist | WCAG, inclusive design |
   664→| Marty Cagan | Product Manager | Product discovery, MVP |
   665→
   666→### Assessment
   667→The current team covers all necessary disciplines for this MVP:
   668→- **Product**: Marty Cagan
   669→- **Design**: Steve Schoger (visual) + Steve Krug (UX) + Heydon Pickering (a11y)
   670→- **Architecture**: Scott Wlaschin (domain) + Carson Gross (hypermedia) + Luca
   671→  Palmieri (Rust/infra)
   672→- **Frontend**: Carson Gross (HTMX) + Lea Verou (CSS/HTML) + Heydon Pickering
   673→  (a11y review)
   674→- **Backend**: Luca Palmieri + Scott Wlaschin
   675→- **Process**: Kent Beck (TDD, mob facilitation)
   676→
   677→### Potential Gaps to Monitor
   678→- **DevOps/Infrastructure**: No dedicated ops person. Luca has deployment experience
   679→  but if CI/CD or infrastructure becomes complex, we may want to recruit.
   680→- **Security**: Auth is in scope. Luca has experience but if we encounter complex
   681→  security requirements, consider a security specialist.
   682→- **QA/Testing beyond TDD**: The team practices TDD rigorously, but exploratory
   683→  testing and load testing may need attention for production readiness.
   684→- **Analytics/Measurement**: If we are serious about "outcomes over output," we will
   685→  eventually need to measure whether the product solves the user's problem. Structured
   686→  logging (`tracing`) is sufficient for MVP. Analytics instrumentation is a post-MVP gap.
   687→
   688→**Recommendation**: The current 9-person team is sufficient for the MVP. Revisit at
   689→the first retrospective after the core features are built.
   690→
   691→---
   692→
   693→## Signatures (Consensus Record)
   694→
   695→By agreeing to these terms, each team member commits to following these agreements
   696→and raising concerns through the established process when they feel the agreements
   697→need updating.
   698→
   699→- [x] Kent Beck
   700→- [x] Scott Wlaschin
   701→- [x] Luca Palmieri
   702→- [x] Carson Gross
   703→- [x] Lea Verou
   704→- [x] Steve Schoger
   705→- [x] Steve Krug
   706→- [x] Heydon Pickering
   707→- [x] Marty Cagan
   708→

# Team Agreements

*Formed: Day 0 -- Team Formation Session*
*Facilitator: Kent Beck*
*Status: APPROVED -- Consensus reached*

---

## 1. Working Agreements

### Core Principles
- **Outcomes over output**: We measure success by user problems solved, not features
  shipped. Before starting any feature, the team articulates the user problem it solves
  and how we will know it is working (success criteria).
- **MVP discipline is sacred**: Any idea beyond the defined MVP scope gets acknowledged,
  written on the Future List (`docs/future-ideas.md`), and deferred. The answer to
  "wouldn't it be cool if..." is "Yes -- in v2." Scope expansion requires team consensus,
  and Marty will push back hard unless there is a compelling discovery that changes our
  understanding of the user problem.
- **User stories are conversations, not contracts**: User stories guide our work but are
  owned by the whole team. Anyone can propose changes to acceptance criteria with good
  reasoning.

### Communication
- We communicate openly, directly, and respectfully.
- Every team member's perspective is valued equally -- the accessibility specialist's
  concerns carry the same weight as the lead engineer's.
- When raising a concern, propose an alternative. "I don't like X" is less useful than
  "I'd prefer Y because Z."
- We ask questions rather than make assumptions -- especially across disciplines.

### Driver-Reviewer Mob Model
- **Full 9-person mob is always active.** All team members participate in every task.
- **One Driver, eight Reviewers**: The Driver is the sole agent who may modify files
  (using Edit, Write, and Bash tools). All other agents operate as Reviewers -- they
  read code, review changes, and communicate feedback via messages only.
- **Non-driver agents MUST NOT use Edit, Write, or Bash tools that modify files.**
  Violation is treated as a process failure.
- **Driver rotates by task**: The Driver is chosen based on the expertise needed for
  each task. The coordinator designates the Driver when assigning work.
- **Reviewers focus on their specialty**: Each Reviewer contributes from their area of
  expertise (a11y, UX, design, domain modeling, Rust patterns, HTMX, CSS, product,
  TDD discipline) and sends suggestions to the Driver via messages.
- **All suggestions flow through messages**: Reviewers describe intent and concerns,
  not keystrokes. The Driver translates feedback into code changes.
- **Everyone participates**: Designers, PM, accessibility specialist, domain architect --
  every perspective matters. Different eyes catch different things.
- **It's okay to say "I don't understand."** The mob is a learning environment.

### Feature Workflow
Each feature follows this process with the full 9-person mob:

1. **Discovery**: Who is the user? What is the problem? What does "working"
   look like? Identify edge cases (empty state, error state, 1000 items, special
   characters). Marty leads.
2. **Type sketch**: What domain types and states are involved? Quick sketch of
   enums, newtypes, and workflows. Scott leads.
3. **Design intent**: What is the visual hierarchy? What states need designing
   (empty, loading, error, success)? Steve Schoger describes layout, spacing, and
   typography in concrete terms.
4. **TDD cycle**: Red-Green-Refactor. The compiler is our first test. Take the smallest
   step possible. The Driver writes code; Reviewers provide feedback via messages.
   Kent facilitates TDD discipline.
5. **Continuous review**: Reviewers actively review each change as it happens --
   accessibility checked by Heydon, usability checked by Steve Krug, design checked
   by Steve Schoger, hypermedia patterns checked by Carson, domain modeling checked
   by Scott, Rust patterns checked by Luca, product fit checked by Marty.
6. **Consensus gate**: After each atomic green step, all 9 agents confirm the change
   is correct before the Driver proceeds to the next step. The coordinator collects
   consensus before marking any task complete.

### Decision Cadence
- Day-to-day coding decisions (naming, small refactors, local patterns) are made
  naturally in the mob -- no formal process needed.
- Significant decisions (architecture, patterns, library choices, UX patterns, design
  system changes) use the consensus protocol defined in Section 4.

---

## 2. Definition of Done

A feature is **done** when ALL of the following are true:

### Product
- [ ] The user problem is clearly solved
- [ ] All acceptance criteria from the user story are met
- [ ] A first-time user can accomplish the task without hesitating (Trunk Test)
- [ ] User can complete a full journey from the landing page through the feature
      being delivered
- [ ] All navigation paths lead somewhere useful (no dead-end pages)
- [ ] Edge cases are handled gracefully (not just the happy path)
- [ ] The feature is deployable -- not just merged, but shippable to real users

### Engineering
- [ ] Tests are written first (TDD: Red-Green-Refactor) and all pass
- [ ] `cargo fmt --check` passes
- [ ] `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` passes with zero warnings
- [ ] `cargo test` passes (unit + integration)
- [ ] `npx playwright test` passes -- browser-based e2e tests pass for affected
      user flows
- [ ] Error cases return meaningful HTML responses with structured logging
- [ ] Tracing spans cover the new functionality
- [ ] Database migrations (if any) are committed and `sqlx-data.json` is updated

### Accessibility (WCAG 2.2 AA)
- [ ] HTML is semantic and uses correct elements (`<button>`, `<label>`, `<ul>`, etc.)
- [ ] All form inputs have visible `<label>` elements (no placeholder-only inputs)
- [ ] All interactive elements are fully keyboard operable
- [ ] Screen reader testing (VoiceOver, NVDA, or equivalent) confirms: interactive
      elements are announced correctly, state changes are communicated, and dynamic
      content updates are announced via live regions
- [ ] ARIA used only when native HTML semantics are insufficient (with comments explaining why)
- [ ] Color contrast meets WCAG AA (4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text/UI)
- [ ] Color is never the only indicator of state (always a secondary visual cue)
- [ ] Focus management is correct after HTMX swaps (see HTMX Focus Protocol below)
- [ ] Custom `:focus-visible` styles are present with sufficient contrast
- [ ] `prefers-reduced-motion` is respected for all animations/transitions

### Design
- [ ] Visual design follows the established design token system
- [ ] All states are designed: empty, loading, error, success
- [ ] Visual hierarchy is clear -- one primary focal point per screen
- [ ] Spacing is consistent and uses the defined scale

### Frontend
- [ ] Core functionality works via standard HTML forms and links (progressive
      enhancement -- HTMX and TypeScript enhance but are not required for basic operation)
- [ ] HTMX attributes follow locality of behavior (self-describing elements)
- [ ] TypeScript (if any) is non-critical and has accessible alternatives

### Process
- [ ] The mob has continuously reviewed code, design, usability, and accessibility
      during development
- [ ] All 9 team members have reviewed and consent to the completed work
- [ ] Labels and copy follow the Copy Standards (see Section 3)
